schools.summary: Chrysostom: nothing is so curious and so fondly prone to pry into things obscure and concealed as the nature of man, especially when the mind is unsettled and in an imperfect state, like the simpler sort of children who never cease teasing their nurses and tutors with frequent questions; Paul checks this idle curiosity about dates; Paul saying that, concerning the times and seasons, they had no need that he write to them — the curiosity about dates checked, the certainty of the event affirmed (Paul, having comforted them about the dead at Christ's coming, turning to the question of 'when,' and declining to feed idle speculation about the precise times and seasons of that day, not because such knowledge would profit them but because the day's certainty, not its date, is what matters, and of the manner of its coming they were already sufficiently taught).

themes: of the times and the seasons, brethren
ye have no need that I write unto you
the curiosity about dates checked
the day's certainty mattering more than its date

schools.summary: Gill: they knew perfectly — with great exactness and accuracy, with great clearness and perspicuity, as a certain truth made plain and evident, about which there could be no question — and this perfect knowledge they had either from the words of Christ himself (Matthew 24:43) or from the ministration of the apostle and his fellow labourers; Paul declaring that they knew perfectly that the day of the Lord comes as a thief in the night — the certainty of the day and the suddenness of its coming (Paul, instead of dates, giving them what they already knew well: that 'the day of the Lord,' the day of his coming and judgment, comes 'as a thief in the night,' suddenly, unexpectedly, and at an hour none can foresee, taking the careless by surprise as a thief breaks in when men sleep, so watchfulness, not date-setting, is the believer's wisdom).

themes: for yourselves know perfectly
the day of the Lord so cometh
as a thief in the night
suddenly and unforeseen, so watchfulness is wisdom

schools.summary: Gill: 'they' are wicked and ungodly men, persons in a state of unregeneracy, who cry 'peace and safety' while careless of the coming judgment (the apostle here referring only to those on whom sudden destruction will come, cf. Matthew 24:36ff; 2 Peter 3:3-4); Paul declaring that when they say 'peace and safety,' then sudden destruction comes upon them as travail on a woman with child, and they shall not escape — the false security of the wicked broken by inescapable ruin (Paul showing the terror the day holds for the unready: just when the ungodly are crying 'peace and safety,' flattering themselves that all is well and no judgment near, 'sudden destruction' falls upon them, as inevitable and as suddenly as the birth-pangs that seize a pregnant woman, allowing no escape, so their very security is the prelude to their ruin, and there is no fleeing the day for those it surprises).

themes: when they shall say, Peace and safety
then sudden destruction cometh upon them
as travail upon a woman with child
they shall not escape

schools.summary: Gill: a state of unregeneracy is a state of darkness, blindness, and ignorance, which is the condition of all men by nature — they are born in darkness, brought up in it, willingly walk in it, are covered with it, and dwell in it (as the Egyptians); but believers are delivered from it; Paul declaring that they, the brethren, are not in darkness, that the day should overtake them as a thief — the believer's deliverance from the darkness that the day surprises (Paul drawing the comforting contrast: unlike the careless wicked who walk in the darkness of unbelief and sin and are caught unawares, the believers 'are not in darkness,' brought into the light of Christ and the knowledge of the truth, awake and watching, so 'that day' shall not 'overtake' them 'as a thief,' surprising and destroying them, for the children of light are forewarned and forearmed).

themes: but ye, brethren, are not in darkness
that that day should overtake you as a thief
the believer's deliverance from surprise
the children of light forewarned and forearmed

schools.summary: Gill: they are enlightened persons, whose understandings were enlightened by the Spirit of God to see their lost state by nature, the exceeding sinfulness of sin, the insufficiency of their own righteousness to justify them, the fulness and excellency of Christ's righteousness, and the way of salvation by Christ; Paul declaring that they are all the children of light and of the day, not of the night nor of darkness — the bright character of all true believers as light's children (Paul pressing the contrast home: all the believers, without exception, 'children of light' and 'children of the day,' born of the light, belonging to the realm of light and truth, enlightened by the Spirit, and emphatically 'not of the night, nor of darkness,' having no part in the ignorance, sin, and false security that mark the unconverted, so their whole nature and standing fit them for the day, not the dark).

themes: ye are all the children of light, and the children of the day
we are not of the night, nor of darkness
the bright character of all believers
enlightened by the Spirit, belonging to the light

schools.summary: Gill: 'let us not sleep as do others' — as the rest of the Gentiles, unconverted persons in a state of darkness, children of the night; let us not act that part or be like them, which professors of religion too much are when they indulge carnal lusts and pleasures and are careless and thoughtless about the coming of Christ; Paul exhorting that they not sleep as others but watch and be sober — the duty of the children of light to watch and be sober (Paul drawing the practical lesson from their bright character: since they are of the day, they must not 'sleep,' live in carnal security, spiritual drowsiness, and indifference, 'as do others,' the unconverted and the careless professor, but must 'watch,' be spiritually alert and on guard for Christ's coming, and 'be sober,' temperate, self-controlled, clear-minded, free from the intoxication of the world's cares and pleasures, as becomes those who belong to the day).

themes: therefore let us not sleep, as do others
but let us watch and be sober
the duty of the children of light
spiritual alertness and sobriety

schools.summary: Gill: the night is the usual season for sleep, and sleep is only for such who are in darkness and children of the night, and not proper to be indulged by children of the day; Barnes: night is the time for sleep, the day the time for action; Paul observing that those who sleep sleep in the night, and those who are drunken are drunken in the night — the works of darkness belonging to the night not the day (Paul supporting his exhortation with a plain truth from common life: sleeping and drunkenness are the deeds of night-time, men sleeping and growing drunk under cover of darkness, and so, by analogy, spiritual sleep, carelessness, and spiritual drunkenness, indulgence in worldly pleasures, belong to the night of unbelief and sin, utterly unfitting for those who are children of the day).

themes: for they that sleep sleep in the night
they that be drunken are drunken in the night
the works of darkness belonging to the night
spiritual sleep and indulgence unfitting for the day

schools.summary: Gill: we who are of the day are to be sober — to cast off the works of darkness and have no fellowship with them, and, since the day of grace has passed upon us and the true light shines, to walk as children of the light, living soberly, righteously, and godly; Paul exhorting that they who are of the day be sober, putting on the breastplate of faith and love, and for a helmet the hope of salvation — the watchful sobriety armed with faith, love, and hope (Paul, returning to the duty of sobriety, arming the watching believer for the day with the great Christian graces cast as a soldier's armour: 'the breastplate of faith and love' guarding the heart, and 'the hope of salvation' as a helmet covering the head, so the three abiding graces, faith, love, and hope, are the very defence by which the child of the day stands ready and unsurprised for the coming of the Lord).

themes: but let us, who are of the day, be sober
putting on the breastplate of faith and love
for an helmet, the hope of salvation
watchful sobriety armed with faith, love, and hope

schools.summary: Gill: God has not appointed his people to wrath, to destruction and ruin (the effect of wrath); though there are some who are vessels of wrath fitted for destruction, of old ordained to condemnation, reserved for the day of evil, there are others who, though equally children of wrath in themselves and as deserving of God's wrath as others, are not appointed to it; Paul declaring that God has not appointed us to wrath but to obtain salvation by our Lord Jesus Christ — the sovereign appointment of his people to salvation not wrath (Paul giving the deepest ground of the believer's hope and security against the day of wrath: God's own eternal appointment, which has destined his people not 'to wrath,' the condemnation and ruin that fall on the ungodly, but 'to obtain salvation,' and that 'by our Lord Jesus Christ,' through his mediation and work, so the believer's escape from the coming destruction rests not on himself but on God's sovereign, gracious purpose in Christ).

debates.question: On what does the believer's escape from the day of wrath rest?

sides.position: On God's sovereign, eternal appointment — he has destined his people not to wrath but to obtain salvation by Christ, so their security rests on God's gracious purpose, not themselves

themes: for God hath not appointed us to wrath
but to obtain salvation by our Lord Jesus Christ
the sovereign appointment to salvation
the believer's escape resting on God's purpose

schools.summary: Gill: Christ died for the elect, who are not appointed to wrath but to salvation by Christ, on which account he died for them — not merely as a martyr to confirm his doctrine, or only by way of example, but as a surety, in the room and stead of his people, as a sacrifice for their sins to make atonement and save them from them; Paul declaring that Christ died for us, that whether we wake or sleep we should live together with him — the death of Christ the ground of the believer's life, in death or life (Paul rooting the appointed salvation in the atoning death of Christ, who 'died for us' in the believer's place as a sacrifice for sin, with this end: that 'whether we wake or sleep,' whether found alive at his coming or already fallen asleep in death, 'we should live together with him,' all his people, living and dead alike, sharing one common life and eternal fellowship with him, so death cannot sever the believer from the life Christ purchased).

themes: who died for us
that, whether we wake or sleep
we should live together with him
death cannot sever the believer from Christ's life

schools.summary: Gill: they were to comfort one another either with the doctrine of the resurrection of the dead, the second coming of Christ, and the thought of being forever with him and one another, or with the consideration that they were not in a state of darkness, ignorance, and infidelity but children of the light, appointed not to wrath but to salvation; Paul charging them to comfort themselves together and edify one another, even as they were already doing — the duty of mutual comfort and building up, already practised (Paul, drawing the practical use from the great truths of salvation and the coming, bidding the believers 'comfort,' console and cheer, one another with these doctrines and 'edify,' build up in faith and holiness, one another, and graciously adding 'even as also ye do,' commending the practice already among them while urging its continuance, so the church is to be a place of mutual encouragement and growth).

themes: wherefore comfort yourselves together
and edify one another
even as also ye do
mutual comfort and building up

schools.summary: Chrysostom: it must needs happen that a ruler should have many occasions of enmity — as physicians are compelled to give much trouble to the sick, preparing diet and medicines not pleasant but attended with benefit, and as fathers are often annoying to their children, so also teachers, and much more; yet, though the physician be odious, it is for benefit; Paul beseeching them to know those who labour among them, are over them in the Lord, and admonish them — the honour due to faithful, labouring pastors (Paul turning to the believers' duty toward their ministers, asking them to 'know,' acknowledge, regard, value, those who serve them, describing such pastors by their threefold work: they 'labour among you,' toil hard in teaching and ministry, are 'over you in the Lord,' set as rulers and overseers by the Lord's appointment in spiritual things, and 'admonish you,' warn, reprove, and instruct, so the people are to recognize and esteem the office and labour of their faithful guides).

themes: to know them which labour among you
and are over you in the Lord
and admonish you
the honour due to faithful, labouring pastors

schools.summary: Gill: they are to be esteemed very highly (or, as the Ethiopic renders, 'honour them abundantly') — for such are worthy of double honour and to be had in reputation, honourably thought of and high in the affections of the saints, who should esteem them better than themselves and speak well of them, for their work's sake; Paul charging them to esteem their pastors very highly in love for their work's sake, and to be at peace among themselves — the loving esteem of ministers and the peace of the church (Paul completing the duty toward pastors: they are to be held in the very highest esteem, and that 'in love,' with warm, heartfelt affection not cold deference, and 'for their work's sake,' because of the worth and importance of the gospel labour they do not for personal reasons, and joining to this the broader charge 'be at peace among yourselves,' that the whole church live in mutual harmony, which is itself bound up with right regard for its leaders).

themes: to esteem them very highly in love
for their work's sake
be at peace among yourselves
loving esteem of pastors and the church's peace

schools.summary: Gill: this is said either to the ministers who laboured among them, presided over them, and admonished them (since some of these things are elsewhere pressed on the members), or to the members in conjunction with their pastors; Paul exhorting them to warn the unruly, comfort the feebleminded, support the weak, and be patient toward all — the wise and tender care suited to each kind of soul (Paul giving a fourfold rule of pastoral and brotherly care, fitting the remedy to the need: the 'unruly,' the disorderly, those out of rank, to be 'warned,' admonished and reproved; the 'feebleminded,' the fainthearted and discouraged, to be 'comforted,' cheered and encouraged; the 'weak,' those weak in faith or grace, to be 'supported,' upheld and borne with; and toward 'all men' to be 'patient,' longsuffering and forbearing, so each soul receives the treatment its case requires).

themes: warn them that are unruly, comfort the feebleminded
support the weak
be patient toward all men
fitting the remedy to each soul's need

schools.summary: Gill: none is to render evil for evil — not an ill word for an ill word, railing for railing, nor an ill action for an ill action — and not to any man whatever, not to an enemy, persecutor, or profane person, as well as not to a brother, a believer in Christ; and the saints should not only guard against this in themselves but watch over one another; Paul charging that none render evil for evil to any man, but ever follow what is good, among themselves and to all — the forbidding of revenge and the pursuit of good to all (Paul forbidding the natural impulse of retaliation: the believer to 'see,' take care, that he renders no 'evil for evil' to 'any man,' not even an enemy or persecutor, but instead 'ever,' always, to 'follow,' pursue and seek after, 'that which is good,' actively doing good, and that not only 'among yourselves,' within the church, but 'to all men,' even those without and enemies, so the Christian overcomes evil not by revenge but by persistent goodness).

themes: see that none render evil for evil unto any man
but ever follow that which is good
both among yourselves, and to all men
overcoming evil by persistent goodness

schools.summary: Gill: this rejoicing is not in a carnal but a spiritual way, with joy in the Holy Ghost, which arises from a view of pardon by the blood of Christ, of justification by his righteousness, and atonement by his sacrifice — not in oneself (as the wicked man rejoices in his wickedness, or the hypocrite and formalist in his profession and reputation), but in Christ; Paul commanding them to rejoice evermore — the call to a constant spiritual joy (Paul, in the briefest of commands, enjoining a perpetual gladness: not a fleeting or worldly mirth but the deep, abiding 'joy in the Holy Ghost' that springs from the believer's standing in Christ, his pardon, justification, and reconciliation, a joy grounded not in changing circumstances but in Christ and his unchanging grace, and therefore to be maintained 'evermore,' in every condition).

themes: rejoice
evermore
the call to constant spiritual joy
grounded in Christ, not circumstances

schools.summary: Gill: this does not mean that saints should be always on their knees, or ever lifting up their hands and vocally calling on God, which is not required and would clash with and break in upon other parts of religious worship and the duties of civil life, and besides would be impracticable (for however willing the spiritual man might be, the body could not bear it); Paul commanding them to pray without ceasing — the call to a constant spirit and practice of prayer (Paul, in another brief but weighty word, enjoining unwearied prayer: not the literal impossibility of praying every moment without pause, but a steady perseverance in prayer, praying regularly and often, never giving it up, and keeping the heart always in a prayerful, dependent frame, ready to call on God at all times, so prayer is the believer's continual breath and habit, not an occasional act).

themes: pray
without ceasing
the call to unceasing prayer
steady perseverance and a prayerful frame of heart

schools.summary: Gill: thanks are to be given to God the Father in the name of Christ, for all things (as the Ethiopic renders it) — for all temporal good things, for our beings and their preservation, for food and raiment and all the mercies of life, for the means of grace, the word and ordinances and the ministers of the gospel, and for spiritual blessings; Paul charging them to give thanks in everything, for this is the will of God in Christ Jesus concerning them — the duty of thanksgiving in all conditions, grounded in God's will (Paul completing the trio of brief commands, rejoice, pray, give thanks, with the call to gratitude that knows no exception: 'in every thing,' in every condition prosperous or adverse, the believer to 'give thanks,' owning God's hand and goodness even in trials, and grounding the duty in the highest authority, 'for this is the will of God in Christ Jesus concerning you,' so a thankful spirit in all things is not optional but God's revealed and gracious will for his people).

themes: in every thing give thanks
for this is the will of God in Christ Jesus concerning you
the duty of thanksgiving in all conditions
God's revealed will, even in trials

schools.summary: Chrysostom: a thick mist, a darkness and cloud is spread over all the earth, and showing this the Apostle said 'we were once darkness' and 'ye are not in darkness'; since there is, so to speak, a moonless night and we walk in it, God has given us a bright lamp, kindling in our souls the grace of the Spirit; but some by an unclean life and unworthy doctrines extinguish that light, like a flame deprived of oil or drenched with water; Paul charging them not to quench the Spirit — the warning against extinguishing the Spirit's light and gifts (Paul, in a vivid figure, warning against putting out the fire of the Spirit: the Holy Spirit, with his grace, gifts, and influences, like a holy flame kindled in the soul and the church, which may be 'quenched,' damped down or extinguished, whether by sin in the life, which grieves and stifles him, or by despising and suppressing his gifts and operations in others, as the gift of prophecy, v.20, so the believer is to cherish, not smother, the Spirit's working).

themes: quench not
the Spirit
the warning against extinguishing the Spirit's flame
quenched by sin or by suppressing his gifts

schools.summary: Gill: 'prophesyings' (or 'prophecies') may mean the prophecies of the Old Testament concerning the first coming of Christ — his person, office, work, obedience, sufferings, death, resurrection, ascension, and session — which, though fulfilled, still have their usefulness (by comparing them with facts the perfection of Scripture is seen); or, more immediately, the gift of prophesying or preaching in the church, which was not to be slighted; Paul charging them not to despise prophesyings — the honour due to the prophetic gift and the preaching of the word (Paul, applying the warning against quenching the Spirit, forbidding the despising of 'prophesyings,' the inspired or gifted utterance of God's truth in the church, whether the foretelling and forth-telling of the prophets or the preaching and expounding of the word, which some, perhaps prizing the showier gift of tongues, were inclined to slight; instead the gift is to be valued and welcomed, for through it the Spirit instructs and edifies the church).

themes: despise not
prophesyings
the honour due to the prophetic gift
through it the Spirit instructs and edifies

schools.summary: Gill: they were to prove all things said by the prophets, all the doctrines they delivered — to hear them (though they have not the gift of tongues or other desirable advantages, not to reject and refuse to hear them, lest many useful men be laid aside and the Spirit of God in them quenched), to try their gifts and attend to their doctrines, yet not implicitly believe but examine all by the standard of the word; Paul charging them to prove all things and hold fast that which is good — the duty of discerning testing joined to firm holding of the good (Paul guarding the welcoming of prophecy, v.20, with the rule of discernment: not to swallow everything uncritically but to 'prove all things,' test and examine all that is taught, weighing it by the Scripture and the Spirit, and, having so tried, to 'hold fast,' keep a firm and steadfast grip on, 'that which is good,' the true and sound, the proven, so openness to teaching is balanced by careful discrimination and tenacious loyalty to the truth).

themes: prove all things
hold fast that which is good
testing all teaching by the Scripture
firm holding of the proven good

schools.summary: Gill: this concerns doctrinal evil — not only open error and heresy are to be avoided but whatever has a show of it, looks like it, carries a suspicion of it, or may be an occasion of or lead to it (wherefore all new words and phrases of this kind should be shunned and the form of sound words held fast); and likewise practical evil, not only from sin but the appearance of it; Paul charging them to abstain from all appearance of evil — the avoidance of evil in every form and semblance (Paul completing his charge to discernment with a sweeping rule of holy caution: the believer to 'abstain from,' keep away from and hold off from, not only evil itself but 'all appearance of evil,' every kind and every show of it, whether in doctrine, anything resembling error or heresy, or in conduct, anything that looks like sin or might give occasion to it, so the Christian, having held fast the good, keeps clear even of the semblance of wrong, guarding both his soul and his testimony).

themes: abstain
from all appearance of evil
the avoidance of evil in every form
keeping clear of even the semblance of wrong

schools.summary: Gill: the apostle follows his exhortations with prayer to God, knowing the weakness and impotency of the saints to receive them and act according to them, and his own insufficiency to impress their minds, and that unless the Lord opened their ears to discipline and sealed instruction to them they would be useless; Paul praying that the God of peace sanctify them wholly, and that their whole spirit, soul, and body be preserved blameless unto the coming of the Lord — the prayer that God himself perfect and keep the whole man (Paul, knowing all his exhortations to holiness are vain without God's own working, turning them into prayer: that 'the very God of peace' would 'sanctify' them 'wholly,' entirely and through and through, and that their 'whole spirit and soul and body,' the complete person in every part of his nature, be 'preserved blameless,' kept faultless, until Christ's coming, the naming of spirit and soul and body marking the entirety of the person God sanctifies and keeps).

debates.question: What does 'whole spirit and soul and body' indicate about man's constitution?

sides.position: Some take it as a threefold (trichotomist) constitution — spirit, soul, and body; others as a fuller naming of the twofold (dichotomist) person; either way it marks the entirety of the whole man God sanctifies and keeps

themes: the very God of peace sanctify you wholly
your whole spirit and soul and body be preserved blameless
unto the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ
God himself perfecting and keeping the whole man

schools.summary: Gill: God calls his people into the fellowship of his Son and to his kingdom and glory, and continues to call them, not only externally by his word but internally by his Spirit and grace; Barnes: their sanctification after all depends on him, and as he has begun a work of grace in their hearts they may depend on his faithfulness to complete it; Paul declaring that faithful is he who calls them, who also will do it — the assurance grounded in God's faithfulness to finish his work (Paul, having prayed for their entire sanctification and preservation, grounding the certainty of it not in themselves but in the character of God: 'faithful is he that calleth you,' the God who effectually called them being faithful to his promise and purpose, 'who also will do it,' will himself accomplish the sanctifying and keeping prayed for, so the believer's perseverance rests on the unfailing faithfulness of the God who began the work).

themes: faithful is he that calleth you
who also will do it
God's faithfulness to finish his work
the believer's perseverance resting on God

schools.summary: Gill: this is added with great beauty and propriety, after the apostle had so earnestly and affectionately prayed for them; and it is directed not to the pastors of the church only but to all the members, whom Paul styles 'brethren' in a spiritual relation, as he often does, requesting that they pray for him and the rest of his fellow labourers; Paul asking the brethren to pray for him — the apostle's humble request for the church's intercession (Paul, having just poured out his own prayer for the Thessalonians, fittingly turning and asking for theirs in return: 'brethren, pray for us,' a request showing both his humility, the great apostle owning his need of even the humblest believer's prayers, and the mutual duty of intercession in the body, the people praying for their ministers as the ministers pray for them).

themes: brethren
pray for us
the apostle's humble request for the church's prayers
the mutual duty of intercession

schools.summary: Gill: this is in opposition to an unchaste and hypocritical kiss; Paul's meaning is that they would salute the members of the church in his name and give his Christian love and affections to them, his view being to recommend to them brotherly love to each other and to stir them up to the mutual exercise of it more and more; Paul charging them to greet all the brethren with a holy kiss — the token of pure Christian affection and unity (Paul, as he often does at the close of his letters, bidding the believers express their mutual love by the customary greeting of that time and place, 'a holy kiss,' a kiss marked by purity and sincerity, opposed to any unchaste or feigned salute, the outward sign of the warm, undissembled brotherly affection that should bind the whole church together, so all the brethren, without exception, are to be embraced in this token of holy love).

themes: greet all the brethren
with an holy kiss
the token of pure Christian affection and unity
the sign of undissembled brotherly love

schools.summary: Gill: this is in the form of an oath, and a very solemn one — 'I adjure by the Lord,' by the Lord Jesus — and shows that oaths may be used on certain and solemn occasions; Barnes: 'I charge you by the Lord' (Greek, 'I put you under oath by the Lord') is equivalent to binding persons by an oath; Paul solemnly charging, by the Lord, that the epistle be read to all the holy brethren — the solemn command for the letter's public reading (Paul, with unusual solemnity, laying it upon them as under an oath, 'by the Lord,' that 'this epistle be read unto all the holy brethren,' that none be left out but the whole church hear it, showing both his sense of the importance and authority of the letter, as carrying the word of the Lord, and his care that all the people, not a few only, should have the benefit of its instruction and comfort).

themes: I charge you by the Lord
that this epistle be read unto all the holy brethren
the solemn command for the letter's public reading
showing its authority and his care that all hear

schools.summary: Gill: this is the apostle's usual salutation in all his epistles and the token of their genuineness; Barnes: the close with the customary benediction of grace (the subscription, that it was written from Athens, being a later addition); Paul ending the epistle with the prayer that the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with them, sealed with 'Amen' — the apostolic blessing of grace to close the letter (Paul, as in all his epistles, closing with the great gospel benediction: the invoking of 'the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ,' his free, unmerited favour with all its fruits and blessings, upon the Thessalonians, a fitting end to a letter so full of grace and comfort, and, as he notes elsewhere, the very token by which his genuine letters might be known, sealed with the solemn 'Amen').

themes: the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you
Amen
the closing benediction of grace
the great gospel blessing ending the letter
